whyHot: The default answer whenever anyone posts a HOSAS build asking 'how do I stop my sticks sliding around' — aluminium clamp mounts are the near-universal recommendation for turning a plain desk into a fixed dual-stick station; the pair for stick+throttle runs ~$200-250.
notesForCatalog: Catalog already lists the Monstertech HOTAS mount but treats mounting as a single SKU. Desk mounting is its own experience category — every HOSAS builder needs it, and the desk-mount vs chair-mount vs rail-mount decision is a natural buyer guide. Add as the anchor of a 'mounting' cluster.

product: Foxx Mount Joystick / Throttle Desk Mount
whyHot: The community's value alternative to Monstertech — USA-made, ~$175/pair (or $87.50 each) vs Monstertech's ~$200-250, repeatedly cited as 'just as good for less' with the tradeoff of a 5-6 week lead time.
notesForCatalog: The budget/value counterpart in any mount comparison. Great for a 'Monstertech vs Foxx' head-to-head. Likely direct-only (no Amazon), so affiliate potential is lower — but strong editorial/authority value.

whyHot: The answer for HOSAS players with no desk, standing desks, or recliner setups — clamps the sticks to the chair arms so they travel with you and sit at a natural, wrist-neutral angle. The MFC Flight Chair Configurator lets you build the exact chair rig.
notesForCatalog: Completely missing from the catalog and it's the single biggest ergonomics unlock for HOSAS — sticks at your sides on the chair beats reaching forward to a desk. Distinct enough from desk mounts to be its own product + its own 'do I mount to desk or chair' guide.

whyHot: Virpil's own space-cockpit guide calls extensions the key HOSAS ergonomics move — pairing a 50/75mm extension on the right MongoosT-50CM3 base (and a Z-Extension on the left) is the recommended dual-stick config for comfortable, precise wrist-neutral throw.
notesForCatalog: A near-invisible-to-newbies accessory that the community swears by. Cheap add-on, high attach-rate to any Virpil/VKB base already in the catalog. Perfect 'you already bought the base, here's the $50 that makes it feel right' upsell.

whyHot: Specifically recommended for converting the left stick to act as a throttle in a HOSAS rig — the offset Z shape angles the left grip for pull/push throttle use. Virpil flags it as the popular pairing with the CM3 base.
notesForCatalog: Underrated, HOSAS-specific part almost no beginner knows exists. Great sleeper content: 'the $40 part that fixes left-stick throttle in HOSAS.' Direct-from-Virpil, so lower affiliate but high authority.

whyHot: The most-named haptics unit in sim communities — ships with a 90W amp, wireless remote, and universal mount, and reviewers consistently call felt low-frequency feedback (engine rumble, quantum spool, hull hits) a genuine immersion upgrade for flight/space sim, not just racing.
notesForCatalog: TACTILE is completely absent from the catalog and it's one of the highest-impact 'game-changer' categories community-wide. High ticket, Amazon-available, strong affiliate. Anchor a whole 'feel your ship' section.

product: Dayton Audio BST-1 / TT25 Puck Bass Shakers
whyHot: The DIY-favorite entry into tactile — cheap Dayton pucks + a spare amp is the recurring 'best bang for buck, do multiple units for localized effects' recommendation before people graduate to ButtKicker.
notesForCatalog: The budget on-ramp to tactile and hugely popular for multi-shaker builds. Pairs with an amp + SimHub/SSA software content. Amazon-available = good affiliate. Great 'start tactile for under $100' piece.

whyHot: The go-to long-session chair recommendation — adjustable lumbar + armrests and dense memory foam make it the community pick for the multi-hour hauls Elite/Star Citizen are known for; armrests also serve as a natural HOSAS mounting surface.
notesForCatalog: Seating is entirely missing. A HOSAS 'long haul to the stars' bench is incomplete without a chair, and chairs are high-ticket with strong affiliate programs. Titan Evo = the mainstream anchor; pairs directly with the chair-mount story.

product: Herman Miller x Logitech G Embody
whyHot: The premium counter-recommendation for people who overheat in foam chairs — mesh/porous back resists heat buildup over long sessions and carries a 12-year 24-hour-use warranty, repeatedly cited as the 'buy once cry once' posture choice.
notesForCatalog: The high-end/ergonomic alternative for a 'best sim chair' comparison. Different value prop than the Titan (mesh + posture vs foam + gaming aesthetics). Good for a two-tier chair recommendation.

whyHot: Ranked #1 for flight/sim immersion in Reddit-data aggregations — a 57" dual-4K mini-LED wraparound is the peak flat-panel cockpit immersion pick for people who don't want VR; the wide field fills peripheral vision for dogfighting and station approaches.
notesForCatalog: Displays are absent from the catalog and are one of the two big immersion forks (ultrawide vs VR). High ticket, Amazon-available. Anchor a 'ultrawide vs VR for space sim' pillar with this as the flat-panel hero.

whyHot: The default recommended entry VR headset for Elite/Star Citizen VR — best price/performance balance, PCVR-capable over Link, and the most common 'just try VR in your ship' answer for people on a budget.
notesForCatalog: VR is the single most-cited 'total game-changer' for cockpit immersion and it's missing entirely. Quest 3 = the accessible anchor. Amazon-available, huge search demand. The 'flat vs VR' decision is prime buyer-guide territory.

whyHot: The enthusiast tier for those who've decided VR is their endgame — Bigscreen Beyond for ultralight comfort on long hauls, Pimax Crystal/wide-FOV for maximum resolution and peripheral fill; both recur as the 'if you're serious about VR space sim' picks.
notesForCatalog: The step-up VR tier for a two-headset comparison. Comfort (Beyond) vs FOV/clarity (Pimax) is a real community debate. High ticket; some sold direct so affiliate is mixed.

whyHot: The gold-standard non-VR head tracker — ~15-18ms response and native support in 120+ titles make it the enduring recommendation for looking around the cockpit on a flat screen; the classic 'best of both worlds if you don't want VR' answer.
notesForCatalog: Head tracking is a whole missing category between 'flat screen' and 'VR.' TrackIR is the branded anchor. Amazon-available, strong intent searches ('TrackIR Star Citizen worth it').

whyHot: The clip-free alternative to TrackIR — no hat clip, adds eye tracking (extended view / target focus), ~20-25ms response. Recurring pick for people who hate wearing a reflector, with the tradeoff of fewer supported titles (~50).
notesForCatalog: The 'no clip, adds eye tracking' counterpart in a TrackIR vs Tobii comparison — a genuinely contested community debate = great content. Amazon-available.

whyHot: The sleeper budget pick repeatedly shared on RSI Spectrum — a $15 phone app + free OpenTrack delivers 6DOF head tracking, and users report tracking range that can exceed TrackIR. The 'try head tracking for the price of an app' entry point.
notesForCatalog: A beloved zero-hardware sleeper — content magnet ('free head tracking for Star Citizen') even if it doesn't monetize directly. Drives affiliate on the phone-mount/tripod accessory and builds authority.

whyHot: The community's favorite value IR solution — pairs with free OpenTrack for comparable or better-than-TrackIR performance at 30-50% less cost; the recurring 'don't pay TrackIR prices' recommendation.
notesForCatalog: The value/DIY tier in a head-tracking roundup. Direct-sold so lower affiliate, but completes the head-tracking price ladder (SmoothTrack < DelanClip < Tobii < TrackIR) — a clean comparison table.

whyHot: The backbone of voice control in Star Citizen/Elite — lets you bark commands ('shields, weapons, quantum') instead of memorizing keybinds; near-universally cited as an immersion + control-density win, and the required host for HCS voice packs.
notesForCatalog: Software is a totally missing dimension of the 'complete setup.' Cheap, beloved, and the on-ramp to the HCS voice-pack ecosystem. Great 'set up voice control in Star Citizen' guide even if monetization is thin.

whyHot: The immersion layer on top of VoiceAttack — professionally voiced AI 'ship computers' that talk back, turning command input into a roleplay crew member. Frequently called a game-changer for solo-pilot immersion.
notesForCatalog: The 'personality' upsell to VoiceAttack. Low ticket but high delight, and completes the voice-control story (software + pack). Editorial value: 'give your ship a crew.'

whyHot: The most-shared keypad for taming Star Citizen's enormous keybind list — analog optical keys, up to 8 keymaps per profile switchable on the fly; RSI community hub has published Tartarus mapping guides.
notesForCatalog: The catalog has Stream Deck + one MFD but no keypad. Keypads are the classic 'I have too many keybinds' fix. Amazon-available, high intent. Anchor a 'Stream Deck vs button box vs keypad' comparison.

whyHot: The cult-favorite ergonomic keypad — finger-tower design with an analog thumbstick; Azeron publishes a dedicated Star Citizen mapping guide and the community treats it as the endgame keybind device for people who go all-in.
notesForCatalog: The boutique/enthusiast keypad with a rabid following — perfect 'sleeper the community loves' entry alongside the mainstream Tartarus. Sold direct + Amazon; distinctive enough to earn its own writeup.

whyHot: The mainstream, cheaper sibling to the Stream Deck XL already in the catalog — 15 LCD keys is the sweet spot most Star Citizen players actually recommend for power management, MFD toggles, and quantum/scanning macros without XL sticker shock.
notesForCatalog: Catalog only has the XL. The 15-key MK.2 is the more-recommended default (better value, enough keys). Add as the accessible tier so the Stream Deck line has good/better options.

whyHot: Repeatedly called 'not just the best gaming headset but a genuinely great planar headphone' — the top immersion/detail pick on r/HeadphoneAdvice, and planar separation is exactly what helps you place other ships and effects spatially in a dogfight.
notesForCatalog: Audio is missing and it's a huge, easy affiliate category. Maxwell = the 'best for immersion' hero. Amazon-available. Anchor a 'best headset for space sim' piece (spatial imaging angle).

whyHot: The 'solid daily-driver' wireless pick that keeps getting recommended for comfort on long sessions and low-hassle wireless — the safe mainstream answer for people who don't want to spend Maxwell money.
notesForCatalog: The value/comfort tier in a headset roundup below the Maxwell. High-volume Amazon seller, strong affiliate. Rounds out a two-tier audio recommendation.

whyHot: The upgrade pedal on RSI SimPits threads — users who move up from Thrustmaster TFRP report far more precise yaw and better body stabilization thanks to the open-heel design; the community's 'if you want pedals, get these' answer.
notesForCatalog: Pedals-for-space is a genuine debated category ('worth it in Star Citizen?') and the catalog has none. VKB Mk.IV = the enthusiast anchor. Good 'are rudder pedals worth it for space sim' guide, and offloading yaw to feet is a real HOSAS technique.

whyHot: The most common first pedal set — the 'good enough to try yaw-on-feet' budget entry that people cite before deciding whether to invest in VKB; the baseline in every rudder-pedal discussion.
notesForCatalog: The budget on-ramp for pedals; Amazon-available with good affiliate. Pairs against VKB Mk.IV for a two-tier pedal comparison ('try yaw-on-feet cheap, then upgrade').

whyHot: The budget battlestation-lighting favorite — huge product range and reactive/immersion modes that mirror on-screen color for a 'ship glow' effect behind the monitor; the value pick over Nanoleaf in most setup threads.
notesForCatalog: Cheap, high-attach 'finishing touch' for a cockpit vibe and a strong Amazon affiliate category. Missing entirely. Content: 'ambient lighting to complete your cockpit,' Govee = value anchor.

whyHot: The premium/aesthetic ambient option — fluid animations and top-tier color accuracy make it the 'showpiece' wall lighting for battlestation builds when Govee's strips aren't dramatic enough.
notesForCatalog: The premium/aesthetic tier of ambient lighting for a Govee-vs-Nanoleaf comparison. Strong Amazon affiliate; completes the 'set the mood' section of a full-cockpit build.

product: Monstertech Cable Management + Under-Desk Tray / Velcro Kit
whyHot: A HOSAS bench has 2 sticks + throttle + pedals + Stream Deck + audio all trailing USB — cable chaos is the recurring complaint once a build is done; Monstertech and generic under-desk trays/velcro are the standard cleanup answer.
notesForCatalog: Nobody's hero item, but every finished build needs it — the 'tidy your 6-device cockpit' accessory. Low ticket, but great as an evergreen accessory cluster (tray + velcro + USB hub) with decent Amazon affiliate.

product: Powered 7-10 Port USB 3.0 Hub (e.g. Anker / Sabrent)
whyHot: The unglamorous necessity — a full HOSAS + Stream Deck + pedals + head tracker + headset dongle setup blows past a motherboard's ports, and a powered hub is the standard fix people only discover after their sticks start dropping out.
notesForCatalog: A genuine gap — multi-device rigs need it and it's a clean Amazon affiliate. 'Powered' matters (unpowered hubs cause dropouts). Pairs naturally with the cable-management cluster.

product: Gel Wrist Rest + Forearm Support Pad
whyHot: Long HOSAS sessions strain wrists when sticks sit above the desk; forearm/wrist support is a recurring comfort recommendation for anyone flying hours a day, especially before they commit to a chair-mount solution.
notesForCatalog: The 'comfort' dimension is missing. Cheap, high-attach, and pairs with the ergonomics story (mount height + extension + wrist support). Low ticket but easy Amazon affiliate and completes the 'fly for hours without pain' angle.

whyHot: Physical switch panels are the buzzy step beyond a Stream Deck — dedicated toggles for power, shields, and landing gear that combine with the WinWing HOTAS ecosystem already popular in the catalog; WINCTRL's modular MIP line is the current build-your-panel-wall trend.
notesForCatalog: Catalog has one Thrustmaster MFD Cougar; the modern trend is modular switch/MIP panels (WinWing/WINCTRL). Extends the WinWing story already in the catalog and feeds the 'physical panels vs Stream Deck' debate.

whyHot: The most-recommended HOSAS grip in Virpil's own space guide — 32-button layout with contactless sensors, available in matched left+right for true dual-stick symmetry; the 'if you're building HOSAS on Virpil bases, run two Alphas' consensus.
notesForCatalog: Catalog has the Constellation ALPHA grip but the ALPHA Prime (contactless, L/R matched) is the current hot HOSAS choice for a symmetric two-stick build. Reinforce the 'buy two matched grips' HOSAS message.
